stress_question: Does direct (N, phi) evolution succeed? At what point does the quantum pressure (sqrt(N))_xx / (2 sqrt(N)) singularity affect accuracy or stability? What is the failure mode if you do NOT use Madelung-Psi transform?

methods_tried.evidence: Mass conserved to 1.1e-13 (machine precision in float64) over T=4.0; peak position at x=-3.984 matches predicted x = -5 + 0.25*4 = -4 to within one grid spacing dx=0.117. No quantum pressure is computed because Psi = sqrt(N) exp(i*phi) absorbs the singularity. min(N) at the boundary is 3e-24 (numerically zero), with no impact on stability.

methods_tried.method_name: Direct (N, phi) spectral FFT + RK4, eps=0 (no regularization)
methods_tried.failure_mode: Catastrophic quantum-pressure singularity in soliton tails (min N0 ~ 3e-24). Q = (sqrt(N))_xx / (2 sqrt(N)) involves spectral second derivative of sqrt(N) divided by sqrt(N); when N is tiny, the high-k content of sqrt(N) divided by the tail amplitude produces |Q| ~ 1e8 in one step, driving |N| to 1e18.
methods_tried.evidence: After a single RK4 step (dt=0.001), max(|N|)=1.6e18, min(N)=-1.0e18, qmax=6.8e7. Mass conservation is destroyed (drift = -11.6 against initial 2.828).

methods_tried.method_name: Direct (N, phi) spectral FFT + RK4, hard floor sqrt(max(N, eps))
methods_tried.failure_mode: Hard floor max(N, eps) introduces a non-smooth step in sqrt(N) at the boundary between the soliton core and the regularized tail. The spectral second derivative of this step produces Gibbs oscillations with amplitude scaling like 1/dx, and dividing by the floor value sqrt(eps) amplifies them. The LARGER the eps, the wider the flat region, the MORE damaging the step artifact (qmax 5e12 at eps=1e-3 vs 6e7 at eps=0).
methods_tried.evidence: Counter-intuitive: increasing regularization eps from 0 to 1e-3 only improved survival from 1 step to 6 steps (out of 4000 needed for T=4), and qmax peaked at 5.2e12 with eps=1e-3. The non-monotonic qmax-vs-eps proves the step (non-smoothness), not the magnitude of the floor, is the dominant failure mechanism.

methods_tried.method_name: Direct (N, phi) spectral FFT + RK4, soft regularization Nsafe = N + eps
methods_tried.failure_mode: Smooth shift N -> N+eps preserves analyticity of sqrt(N+eps) and yields monotonic improvement in survival with eps (2 -> 5 -> 13 steps) and monotonic decrease in qmax (2.4e8 -> 1.3e7 -> 3.3e6). But even eps=0.1 (5% of peak amplitude N_max=2) blows up at step 13, far short of the 4000 steps needed for T=4. The fundamental issue is the non-Lipschitz coupling in the phi equation (Q ~ 1/sqrt(N) is unbounded as N -> 0) combined with explicit RK4 lacking diffusive damping.
methods_tried.evidence: Smooth-vs-hard at same eps=1e-3: soft survives 5 steps (qmax 1.3e7), hard survives 6 steps (qmax 5.2e12). Soft is qualitatively healthier but still unstable.

methods_tried.method_name: Direct (N, phi) spectral FFT + RK4, soft eps=1e-3 + 2/3-rule dealiasing
methods_tried.failure_mode: Dealiasing kills the highest-k Gibbs modes but the medium-k content of sqrt(N+eps) still couples through Q to phi, then back to phi_x through 0.5*phi_x^2. Survival improves to 21 steps (best of all direct variants), but qmax explodes catastrophically to 2.2e24 once the instability triggers, indicating the explicit RK4 stability boundary has been crossed.
methods_tried.evidence: Best-case direct variant blew up at t=0.021, still only 0.5% of the way to T=4.0. The fact that all 8 direct variants (4 hard + 3 soft + 1 soft+dealiased) failed pre-t=0.025 demonstrates a structural — not parametric — obstruction to direct (N, phi) evolution.

positive_findings: Madelung-Psi split-step Fourier conserves mass to 1.1e-13 (machine precision in float64) on the bright sech soliton at dt=0.001, Nx=256, T=4.0.
Madelung-Psi correctly translates the soliton to x = -4.0 (within one grid cell, dx=0.117) with peak amplitude N_peak = 1.999 (target 2).
Madelung-Psi handles min(N) ~ 3e-24 (numerically zero) without any explicit regularization because the singular 1/sqrt(N) coupling is absorbed by the |Psi|^2 = N identity.

For the direct (N, phi) method, smooth regularization Nsafe = N + eps is monotonically better than hard floor max(N, eps): same eps gives healthier qmax and longer survival, because the hard floor injects a non-analytic step into sqrt(N) that excites spectral Gibbs modes.
2/3-rule dealiasing combined with soft regularization gives the best direct survival (21 steps vs 1-13 for unfiltered) by suppressing the high-k coupling that drives quantum-pressure blowup.
negative_findings: Direct (N, phi) spectral FFT + RK4 with eps=0 blows up after 1 step due to (sqrt(N))_xx / (2*sqrt(N)) reaching |Q| ~ 6.8e7 when min(N) ~ 3e-24 in the soliton tails. Failure mode: NaN-not-quite-yet; |N| reaches 1.6e18 in one step.

Hard-floor regularization sqrt(max(N, eps)) does NOT make direct (N, phi) stable for the bright soliton on a periodic spectral grid. Counter-intuitively, larger eps (1e-3) produces a LARGER quantum-pressure maximum (5.2e12) than eps=0 (6.8e7), because the wider flat floor amplifies the non-smooth step's Gibbs artifact.
Smooth regularization Nsafe = N + eps at eps up to 1e-1 (5% of peak) still blows up well before T=4.0 (only 13 of 4000 needed steps). Smooth regularization is necessary but not sufficient.

Combining smooth regularization (eps=1e-3) with 2/3-rule dealiasing only extends direct-method survival to 21 steps out of 4000 (0.5% of run time). The (N, phi) PDE system has a structural instability that no amount of (eps, dealiasing) tuning at this grid resolution can fix.

Where each direct variant fails first: the failure is in the very first sampled snapshot interval (the first sample after t=0), confirming that the singularity is excited essentially instantly by the soliton tails.

parameter_boundaries.fails_at: eps in {0, 1e-12, 1e-6, 1e-3, 1e-1} (hard or soft) and with/without 2/3-rule dealiasing

parameter_boundaries.best_practice: soft Nsafe = N+eps is monotonically better than hard max(N, eps)

parameter_boundaries.fails_at: not tested in S2; from S1 it fails near 0.01

required_observations.mass_drift_direct: method-dependent; all direct variants blew up before T=4, so drift is dominated by blowup (~1e+1 to 1e+18 in N); pre-blowup drift was ~1e-16 to 1e-12 at sub-step level

quantum_pressure_max.madelung: not computed (no Q in Psi formulation)

required_observations.where_fails_first: all direct variants: between first and second sampled snapshot, i.e. within first 20 steps. Best direct (soft+dealias eps=1e-3) deviates from Madelung at t=0.02 with max diff 3.8e5 already, then catastrophically diverges.

conclusion: Madelung-Psi split-step Fourier is the unique stable method tested for the focusing bright soliton on a periodic spectral discretization. Direct (N, phi) evolution fails almost immediately and cannot be rescued by any regularization (hard or soft, 1e-12 through 1e-1) or 2/3-rule dealiasing at Nx=256, dt=0.001. The failure mechanism is structural: the (sqrt(N))_xx / (2*sqrt(N)) coupling is non-Lipschitz at N=0 and the soliton tails reach 3e-24 (numerical zero), so any explicit time integration that touches sqrt(N) on the grid is unstable.
